w: The only museum in the world to Maria Skłodowska-Curie sits in the New Town house where she was born on 7 November 1867 — her mother once ran a girls' boarding school here. The Polish Chemical Society opened it in 1967, her centenary, with the family's blessing. The double Nobel laureate, finally at home.

Within these walls Emanuel Ringelblum's secret 'Oneg Shabbat' circle documented the Warsaw Ghetto, then buried their archive in milk cans — dug up in 1946 and 1950. The building stood beside the Great Synagogue the SS blew up in May 1943; scorch marks from that fire still mark the hall floor, never reconstructed.

A wooden village of 1952, thrown up on Jelonki to house the 4,500 Soviet workers raising the Palace of Culture across town. Prefab timber came in two grades — hostel pavilions for labourers, single-family 'Finnish houses' for engineers. When the Palace was finished it became student dorms. Much of the timber still stands.

Ten tons of bronze winged victory, sword over her head — and the face belongs to Anna 'Hania' Tarczyńska, a real girl from Grochów. Marian Konieczny won the 1959 competition with it, his first major work, beating a hundred rivals. The sword alone weighs a tonne, steel-reinforced to flex 15 cm in a high wind. Warsaw's own Nike.

The Gestapo seized this ministry building on Szucha Avenue in October 1939 and turned its cellar into a holding pen. Four group cells and ten isolation cells survive almost untouched; prisoners were packed onto benches in cells the Germans called 'tramwaje' — trams — up to a hundred interrogated in a day. A mausoleum since 1952.

Not a general or a saint but five backyard buskers, cast life-size in brass on a Praga street corner — violin, accordion, guitar, banjo, drum. Andrzej Renes sculpted them; Archbishop Głódź, newly an honorary citizen, chose to honour ordinary Praga over any famous name. Text a code and it once played one of a hundred old Warsaw songs.

Open since 1930 and still running — Warsaw's oldest working cinema, and the first in the city built for the talkies. It came through the war and reopened in September 1945 with the Soviet film 'Berlin.' The blue rooftop neon spelling ATLANTIC went up after a late-1950s revamp. Four halls, 794 seats, ninety-odd years on Chmielna.

A bronze dog sits in Pole Mokotowskie gazing up at an owner who isn't there — the Happy Dog, modelled on Lokat, a golden-retriever therapy dog. Bogna Czechowska unveiled it on World Animal Day, 2004, during a dog parade thrown by a magazine called Four Paws. A small monument to being a well-loved pet.

A heap of the city's own ruins, grassed over: Warsaw's second-highest point. After the war, rubble from bombed townhouses — some cleared to make room for the Palace of Culture — was piled here in Wola and landscaped into a park hill, 130 metres up. A ski lift ran down it into the 1980s. The dead weight of the city, underfoot.

The city's museum threads through a whole row of Old Town townhouses on the Market Square — the Baryczków house and its neighbours, bought up by the city in the 1930s. Flattened in the war, they were rebuilt in the great 1948–54 reconstruction that won the Old Town its UNESCO listing. A city told through its own everyday things.
